text: The surgeon demonstrates a robotic inguinal hernia repair, focusing on dissecting the preperitoneal pockets. The procedure begins in the parietal compartment medially, then moves laterally to the visceral compartment. The surgeon addresses minor bleeding on the right side while maintaining a thin flap laterally. Dissection continues into the visceral compartment to avoid medializing the cord element and to protect the lateral sidewall. The retropubic space is developed to create a flat area for mesh placement.

The surgeon reduces a small hernia sac and focuses on parietalizing the cord structures to establish the critical view of the myopectineal orifice. An adequate retropubic dissection is performed, creating at least two centimeters of space between the bladder and pubis, extending beyond the midline. The surgeon parietalizes the vascular structures by reflecting down the peritoneum and grabs the medial umbilical ligament to parietalize the vas. Fatty tissue is distinguished from femoral lymph nodes, and dissection around the obturator space creates a healthy space for mesh placement.

The surgeon identifies the obliterated umbilical artery but decides not to transect it. Lateral dissection is performed to confirm the absence of a cord lipoma and identify the transversalis sling. The surgeon exchanges scissors for a mega suture cut to place a mid-weight polypropylene mesh and close the flap. The mesh is positioned carefully, ensuring the pocket is slightly smaller to prevent mesh movement.

The surgeon demonstrates the closure of the flap from lateral to medial, ensuring the medial aspect has no redundancy. The mesh and flap move independently, indicating proper pocket sizing. Absorbable V-lock sutures are used for closure.
